channel.title: +09 Appendix on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः
channel.description: Recent content in +09 Appendix on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः

item.title: 1 Loations
item.description:
Appendix 1

Location of Places Mentioned in Interviews

Places (with variant modern spellings) are located in the relevant district (2020) of Tamil Nadu, moving generally clockwise from northeast to the south and northwest. A more precise location of these places can be found by searching for the village name on the Internet or the name plus the word ‘map’. Otherwise, one can search for the district and then for the place. One can also find maps of the 108 divyadeśas on the Internet.

Tiruppāṉ

Madhurakavi

Tenth century Nāthamuni Eleventh century Yāmuna Twelfth century Rāmānuja Twelfth century Empār

330

Āḻvārs and Early Ācāryas

Twelfth century Mutaliyāṇṭāṉ Twelfth century Piḷḷāṉ Twelfth century Kūreśa (Kūrattāḻvāṉ) Twelfth century Parāśara Bhaṭṭar Thirteenth century Nañjīyar Thirteenth century Nampiḷḷai Thirteenth century Periyavāccāṉ Piḷḷai Thirteenth century Vaṭakku Tiruvīti Piḷḷai Thirteenth–fourteenth century Piḷḷai Lokācārya Thirteenth–fourteenth century Aḻakīya Maṇavāḷa Perumāḷ

item.title: 3 Caste Definitions
item.description:
Appendix 3

Changing Definitions of Caste

As Nicholas Dirks has argued, we must separate the earlier caste system from the influence of colonial thought. The latter assumed that caste was a pervasive, totalising, and uniform system. He reminds us that before the colonial period, caste was not the key category or symbol of Indian civilisation, not even a system; it was merely one expression among many of social identities. Others included ‘temple communities, territorial groups, lineage segments, family units, royal retinues, warrior sub-castes, “little” kingdoms, occupational reference groups, agricultural or trading associations, devotionally conceived networks and sectarian communities …’.1

item.title: 4 Colonial Anti-Brahmanism
item.description:
Appendix 4

The Colonial Cauldron of Anti-Brahmanism

Ferment in the colonial period created a powerful mix of forces that would eventually culminate in the non-Brahmin movement. One commonality among these forces was anti-Brahmanism.

Already in the nineteenth century, non-Brahmins had

begun to protest exclusionary caste practices1 in Madras Presidency. This new self-consciousness was abetted by missionary critiques of the abuse, superstition, hypocrisy, and cunning of Brahmin priests. One of the first to adopt anti-Brahminism was Abbé J. A. Dubois (1765–1848). In Hindu Manners, Customs, and Ceremonies (1806), he praised Jains on the one hand and criticised Brahmins on the other. A more scholarly basis for anti-Brahminism was the concept of the Dravidian. In A Comparative Grammar of the Dravidian or South Indian Family of Languages (1856), Rev. Robert Caldwell (1814– 91) showed through linguistic and other methods that Tamil belonged to a family of languages that he called Dravidian. Caldwell then extended the concept of Dravidian to include culture and religion. He contrasted that with Aryan culture and religion, that is, Brahmanical culture and Advaita Vedānta, especially its modern version, known as neo-Vedānta. The

item.title: 5 Śrīvaiṣṇavism’s Future
item.description:
Appendix 5

Śrīvaiṣṇavism’s Future?

It is hard to predict what Śrīvaiṣṇavism might be like in another twenty years. Conceivably, this could go in several directions. One direction is a general decline of Ś r ī vaiṣṇ avism. The reasons that made it a minority sectarian tradition in Tamil Nadu throughout its history might contribute to such a decline. A few reasons, however, are new. People told me, for instance, that Śrīvaiṣṇavism demanded too much of people. ‘The dress of Tamils has changed,’ said K. K. A. Venkatachari.

item.title: 6 Nadars on Discrimination
item.description:
Appendix 6

Two Nadars on Discrimination

At a Tamil workshop in Puducherry, I met two researchers with PhDs, T. Rajarethinam Chettikulam and M.Prabhakaran. After I gave a talk on my project on non-Brahmins, they told me how their experience differed from my description. They were from the Kanyakumari district, the southernmost part of Tamil Nadu, which borders the modern state of Kerala (about 60 per cent of Nadars speak Malayalam). Although they are not Śrīvaiṣṇavas, they are a kind of Vaiṣṇava that has links with Śrīvaiṣṇavism. For instance, the personal form of the supreme is Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇ, who is also called Perumāḷ. Despite the risk of digression, I think the history of discrimination against this low-caste Vaiṣṇava community and its reaction is an important example of how some Tamil Vaiṣṇavas reacted to discrimination in one region of Tamil Nadu that is related to, and yet very different from, the Śrīvaiṣṇava case.

item.title: 8 Primary Sources
item.description:
Primary Sources, Works in Tamil and Dictionaries

Aḻakiyamaṇavāḷa-rāmānuja-ekaṅki-svāmikaḷ, 1968. Śrīvaiṣṇava Tīpikai,

2 Parts. Madras: K. Nārāyaṇa Reṭṭiyar.

Aṟumukaṉ, K., 1984 [1966]. Tirunārāyaṇatāca Suvāmikaḷ Varalāṟu.

V īrapparaṇṭi: Nārāyaṇatāca Cuvāmikaḷ Tēvattānam.

Inscriptions of Sadasivaraya’s Time: From 1541 to 1547, 1998,

V. Vijayaraghavacharya (trans. and ed.), Tirumala Tirupati

Devasthanams Inscriptions, Vol. V. Tirupati: Tirumala Tirupati

Devasthanams.

Irātākiruṭṭiṇaṉ, M. [Pāvalar Maṇī Cittaṉ]. ‘Vaiṇavattiṉ Etirkālam’, in

Symposium on Sri Vaishnavism Tomorrow, 62–71. Chennai: Bysani

Madhavachetty’s Charity Fund.
